title: History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita
subtitle: Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]
caption:
History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita

Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]

Across the period from 1000 to 2018, real gross domestic product per capita for selected countries remains near the bottom of the scale for most of the timeline before rising steeply in the modern era. The vertical axis measures income in 2011 US dollars adjusted for Purchasing Power Parity, ranging from $0 to $40,000. For nearly eight centuries, the lines for Japan, the United Kingdom, Italy, China, and India cluster tightly within the lowest band, generally staying below $5,000. Italy shows slight fluctuations between $2,000 and $4,000 from 1300 to 1700, while the United Kingdom and Japan remain flatter and lower until the 1800s. Beginning around 1800, the United Kingdom line begins a gradual ascent, followed by Japan after 1870, both accelerating sharply after 1950. By 2018, Japan reaches just under $40,000, the United Kingdom approaches $38,000, and Italy settles near $35,000. China and India remain near the baseline until the late 20th century; China’s line rises visibly after 1980 to reach approximately $15,000 by 2018, while India’s climbs more slowly to about $7,000. The data shows that all five countries experienced minimal growth in per capita income for over 800 years, followed by rapid increases concentrated in the last two centuries — with the steepest gains occurring after 1950 for the earliest industrializers and after 1980 for the later risers. The most visible pattern is the prolonged flatness across all series until the modern period, followed by divergent but universally upward trajectories in the final segment of the timeline.
